header.title: Missed medication — what to do, reporting

body.summary: A missed dose is not always a crisis — but how you respond and record it makes all the difference.
body.text:
## Key points

- As soon as you realise a dose was missed, check the MAR chart and note the time it was due
- Tell your manager straight away — never decide on your own whether to give the dose late or skip it
- Some medicines can be given within a short window; others must not be given late at all — always check with the GP or pharmacist
- Record the missed dose on the MAR chart clearly, including the reason (for example asleep, refused, unavailable, or staff oversight)
- Keep observing the service user for symptoms linked to the missed medicine — especially for insulin, epilepsy, heart, or blood-thinning medication

## Quick decision prompt

- **Missed by less than an hour?** → Contact your manager before doing anything — a late dose may be appropriate for some medicines, but only with guidance
- **Missed by several hours or a whole medication round?** → Do not double up or "catch up" — contact your manager and seek GP or pharmacist advice
- **Service user was asleep or out of the room?** → Record why the dose was missed, inform your manager, and follow your service's procedure for follow-up at the next safe opportunity
- **You only discovered the miss at handover?** → Tell the incoming manager immediately and complete an incident report if your policy requires it

## Common mistakes

- Giving two doses at the next round to make up for a missed one — this can cause serious harm and is never acceptable without written clinical advice
- Leaving the MAR chart blank or signing as given when the medicine was not administered
- Delaying escalation because "it's only one tablet" — some missed doses are low risk, others are not, and you cannot judge that alone
- Assuming a colleague has already reported it — always confirm and document your own actions

## Who to tell

- **Your manager** — immediately, before the end of your shift
- **GP or pharmacist** — if the medicine is high risk, time-critical, or significantly delayed
- **111 or 999** — if the service user shows symptoms that may be linked to the missed dose
- **Family or next of kin** — your manager will advise when this is appropriate
